### **Day 4: SQL Injection \- Login Bypass**

**Challenge:** SQL injection vulnerability allows authentication bypass through direct string concatenation in SQL queries. The flag is stored in the flag\_user account's email address   
.

**Methodology**

1. I went to the login page. The first thing I tried was plugging a single quote **‘** into the username field, and I got the error you can see in the image below. Inserting a lone quote character is an old trick to test if user input is handled properly. As you can see I got an error message which tells me that I can try to further exploit this input field.  
   Whatever you input gets wrapped in quotes by the by the backend before reaching the database, and so by adding an extra quote **‘** you create an unmatched quote that triggers the syntax error.

![][image1]

2. The next step is to craft the payload pluginside the login. We know that the username is flag\_user, and after completing the HTB SQL Injection Fundamentals module I understood how to structure this kind of attack. So the back end looks something similar to this:   
   ![][image2]  
     
   We want to inject into the username field something that will bypass the authentication. Start with **flag\_user**, followed by a single quote **‘**  to close that block (since we start off inside {username}). Then comes the **AND** and to prevent that from ever executing we comment out the end of the payload **\--**. So the final payload is,  
   **flag\_user’ \--**   
   **Image showing being logged in**  
3. Now I could not find the flag inside the flag\_user’s dashboard or locate the email address, but I logged in as the Admin and located the flag on the admin’s dashboard instead. On [Day 4](https://medium.com/@bscsaki/day-4-broken-access-control-admin-panel-8bb0dd08d606) I show you how to login in as the Admin.

**The why**

This is in band SQL injection, specifically error based SQL injection combined with an authentication bypass. In band means the attacker sees results directly in the application's own response, either through returned data, a success message, or a leaked error. This vulnerability is known as [CWE 89](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/89.html) Improper neutralisation of special elements used in an SQL command. When the back end sees **user\_flag’ \--**  followed by my comment marker, my quote closes the username string exactly where the application expected the username value to end, which kept the WHERE clause searching specifically for that one username rather than matching every row. Everything after my comment marker, including the AND password check, was then treated as a comment by the database engine and never evaluated at all. That is why the payload works with literally any password typed alongside it, the check for a matching password never runs. 

**Check out thee labs to practice [All labs | Web Security Academy](https://portswigger.net/web-security/all-labs#sql-injection)** 

**How to prevent**

OWASP suggests these first 4 primary defenses and a few more,

- **Use of Prepared Statements with Parameterized Queries**. This way the user input and prepared statements get sent as 2 separate things to the database instead of a merged string.  
- **Construct Stored Procedures**. These are predefined SQL routines stored on the database server itself, called only with parameters rather than built dynamically from raw input each time. Since the SQL logic is fixed ahead of time on the server, there is no string concatenation step for an attacker's input to hijack.   
- **Allow-list Input Validation**. Rather than trying to block specific bad characters or patterns, this defines exactly what a valid input is allowed to look like   
- **Escaping All User Supplied Input**, this is a last resort and it involves manually neutralizing characters rather than structurally separating code from data.  
- **Keep up with patches, updates and ship fixes** for newly discovered injection vectors.  
- **Utilize the principle of least privilege**, so users have access to the fewest possible tables, and actions.  
- **Do not share Database accounts** between applications to prevent cross platform enumeration.  
- **Configure proper error reporting and handling** on the server so that messages like the error from today’s challenge don’t show up on the front 

**Summary:**

In this challenge of [Certified Vibe Hacker Workshop](https://certifiedvibehacker.com/) by [Hacker Sidekick](https://hackersidekick.com/) we saw an example of an SQL injection attack, where we were given the username and had to craft the payload to give us access as that user.

[SQL Injection | OWASP Foundation](https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/SQL_Injection)

[What is SQL injection (SQLi)? | Tutorial & examples | Snyk Learn](https://learn.snyk.io/lesson/sql-injection/?ecosystem=java)   
[SQL Injection](https://www.w3schools.com/sql/sql_injection.asp)   
[How to Protect Against SQL Injection Attacks | Information Security Office](https://security.berkeley.edu/education-awareness/how-protect-against-sql-injection-attacks)
